title: The Rise of the Neo-Primes in Defense Technology
source: The Guardian - The Economist (Web Edition) 25 April 2026
content:
The world is rearming rapidly, with global military spending seeing its largest inflation-adjusted leap since the Cold War. As European NATO members scramble to expand their armories, they must recognize that it is advanced arms, not just budgets, that truly deter. Recent conflicts demonstrate the pressing need to balance high-end systems with numerous, easily replaceable weapons like low-cost drones. Regardless of a weapon’s size or cost, what increasingly dictates its battlefield effectiveness is the sophisticated software inside. The most capable weapons are those infused with cutting-edge algorithms, trained on superior data, and updated frequently.

America’s defense industry is currently undergoing a dramatic transformation driven by innovative technology firms. Companies like Palantir, SpaceX, and Anduril have emerged as 'neo-primes,' fiercely competing with legacy armsmakers who arrived late to the software revolution. These agile upstarts behave like software-first tech companies and invest heavily in rigorous in-house research and development. To successfully manufacture modern weapons, they have smartly adopted modern production methods inspired by the commercial automotive industry. By heavily prioritizing speed and software integration, these new players are reshaping how the Pentagon acquires and deploys its military technology.

Unfortunately, Europe lags far behind in cultivating its own dynamic defense-tech ecosystem. Small and innovative European firms remain stifled by a highly fragmented market, limited venture capital, and surprisingly low government demand. For example, nearly all of Germany’s massive special defense fund will be spent on traditional, outdated legacy equipment. If Europe fundamentally rebalances its spending and reforms its bureaucratic procurement processes, its native firms might eventually compete globally. Fostering a robust defense-tech industry in Europe is absolutely vital to ensuring the long-term security of the entire NATO alliance.
